questions.question: In the BB84 protocol, what happens when Alice and Bob use different measurement bases?
questions.explanation: When Alice prepares in one basis (e.g., rectilinear) and Bob measures in a different basis (e.g., diagonal), Bob's result is completely random—50% chance of each outcome. This is because the bases are conjugate (mutually unbiased).

questions.question: What is the sifting efficiency of the six-state protocol?
questions.explanation: The six-state protocol uses three mutually unbiased bases. Alice and Bob independently choose from three options, so they match only 1/3 of the time, giving approximately 33% sifting efficiency.

questions.question: What is unique about the B92 protocol compared to BB84?

options.text: It uses only two non-orthogonal states

questions.explanation: B92 uses only two non-orthogonal states (|0⟩ and |+⟩) instead of BB84's four states. This simplifies the source requirements, though it reduces sifting efficiency to about 25%.

questions.question: Why does the six-state protocol have a higher QBER threshold than BB84?

options.text: Eve's information is more constrained with three bases

questions.explanation: With three mutually unbiased bases, an eavesdropper has only a 1/3 chance of guessing the correct basis (vs. 1/2 for BB84). This means Eve introduces more disturbance per bit of information gained, allowing tolerance of ~12.6% QBER vs. BB84's ~11%.

questions.question: In B92, when is Bob's measurement considered 'conclusive'?

options.text: When Bob measures in the same basis as Alice
When Bob's result could only come from one of Alice's states

questions.explanation: In B92, Bob measures in a basis opposite to what Alice might have used. A conclusive result occurs when the outcome could only have come from one of Alice's two possible states—e.g., getting |1⟩ in Z-basis can only happen if Alice sent |+⟩.

questions.question: What is the secret key rate formula's main components for P&M protocols?

options.text: Raw rate × sifting efficiency × (1 - error correction - privacy amplification costs)

questions.explanation: The secret key rate is: R = (raw detection rate) × (sifting efficiency) × (secret fraction). The secret fraction accounts for error correction overhead [f×h(e)] and privacy amplification [h(e)] to remove Eve's information.

questions.question: BB84 was the first prepare-and-measure QKD protocol and remains the most widely implemented today.
questions.explanation: True. BB84, introduced by Bennett and Brassard in 1984, was the first QKD protocol of any type. Its balance of security, efficiency, and implementation simplicity makes it the most commonly deployed protocol in commercial QKD systems.
